living-concrete.knowledge:
Obsidian natively indexes these YAML frontmatter fields: - `tags:` - Appears in tags pane, searchable, clickable - `aliases:` - Alternative names for the note (search, linking) - `cssclasses:` - Custom styling classes
All other frontmatter fields are stored but NOT indexed by Obsidian core. They ARE queryable via Dataview plugin.

Any frontmatter field can be queried with Dataview: ```dataview TABLE status, version FROM "books" WHERE status = "DRAFT" ```
This enables app-specific metadata (status, version, author, dates) without polluting Obsidian's native index.

| Syntax | Obsidian | GitHub | VS Code | Others | |--------|----------|--------|---------|--------| | `[Text](path.md)` | Works | Works | Works | Works | | `[[wikilink]]` | Works | Broken | Broken | Broken |
Standard markdown links are the only portable choice.
living-concrete.pattern:
Structure frontmatter in two sections: ```yaml --- # Obsidian-indexed (native search, tags pane) tags: [concept, architecture] aliases: ["The Pattern", "Design Pattern"]
# App-specific metadata (Dataview-queryable) status: DRAFT version: 1.0 created: 2026-01-26 --- ```
Comment separators optional but aid human readability.

Always use standard markdown links: - `[Chapter 1](./chapter-1.md)` - relative paths - `[API Reference](../api/reference.md)` - cross-directory - `[External](https://example.com)` - URLs
Never use wikilinks (`[[note]]`) in portable documents.

Tags belong in frontmatter, not prose: ```yaml tags: [architecture, pattern, core] ```
NOT inline: `This is about #architecture and #patterns`
Frontmatter tags are cleaner, more queryable, and don't clutter reading.
living-concrete.antipattern: `[[wikilinks]]` and `[[note|alias]]` syntax. Beautiful in Obsidian, renders as broken text everywhere else. GitHub shows literal brackets. VS Code shows nothing. Violates portability-first principle.
`#tags` scattered through prose. Clutters reading experience, harder to query than YAML, and some markdown renderers display them as headings or literals.

Features that break portability: - `![[embedded notes]]` - broken outside Obsidian - `%%comments%%` - Obsidian-specific - `> [!callout]` - Some renderers support, many don't
Use standard markdown equivalents when portability matters.
